purpose: Uniformly-hard version: a reflex-defeated (or non-decorative-routed) answer on EVERY question, to confirm the difficulty knob goes all the way. Compare to batch 1 (3 easy). Facts web-verified. Also stress-tests the downside: does maxing the knob tip from reasoning-hard into obscurity-hard?

candidates.q: Which college football program has won more games than any other — the only one ever to reach 1,000 wins?
candidates.fair_or_obscure: fair — a blue-blood bet you can reason

candidates.q: Players from which school have won the Heisman Trophy more often than from any other?
candidates.note: 8 winners (Bush reinstated 2024); three schools tied at 7.

candidates.q: Which head coach won more games than anyone in major college football history?
candidates.reflex: beats Bear Bryant / Nick Saban (by fame)
candidates.fair_or_obscure: FAIR (corrected) — reasoning heuristic is longevity: 'who coached forever and won?' -> Paterno (46 yrs) and Bobby Bowden (~40 yrs, 377) are the finalists
candidates.note: 409 wins (FBS record). Gagliardi has more across all divisions (D-III); flag in reveal.

candidates.q: In the most lopsided game in college football history, how many points did the winning team score?
candidates.fair_or_obscure: borderline — 'guess high' is reasoning, the exact 222 is recall
candidates.note: Georgia Tech 222, Cumberland 0 (1916). Better as 'closest to 100, 200, or 300?'

candidates.q: In which decade was the first-ever college football game played, between two New Jersey schools?
candidates.reflex: non-decorative route — year not given; derive from the first game

candidates.q: Which SEC school plays in the largest stadium in the conference?
candidates.fair_or_obscure: leans knowledge, but the close race (Kyle Field 102,733 vs LSU 102,321 vs Neyland 101,915) is real doubt
candidates.note: Kyle Field, 102,733 — narrowly the SEC's biggest.

candidates._difficulty_audit: Knob confirmed: 0 easy questions (batch 1 had 3). CORRECTED after user notes: most of these are reasoning-FAIR, not obscurity — each record has a heuristic that reaches the finalists (longevity -> Paterno/Bowden; blue-blood -> Michigan; post-war era -> 1860s; guess-high -> 222; recent expansion -> Texas A&M). True obscurity would be asking the exact figure (409) or a record with no heuristic. The bigger missing lever: SLICERS/FILTERS that remove the obvious answer -- the controllable difficulty dial (e.g. 'most golds among never-host countries'). Difficulty is a dial turned by slicing the obvious, not a canonical-vs-weird binary; still aim for a SPREAD across a real set.
